market_session: US regular session close on 2026-06-22 (first session after the Juneteenth three-day weekend)

calculation.method: reported close and daily move from AP closing-index ledger; prior session was 2026-06-18 because 2026-06-19 was the Juneteenth holiday

text: US stocks finished mixed on Monday, June 22, 2026. The S&P 500 fell 27.79 points (0.4%) to 7,472.79; the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 148.01 points (0.3%) to 51,712.71; the Nasdaq Composite fell 351.33 points (1.3%) to 26,166.60; and the Russell 2000 rose 24.64 points (0.8%) to 3,004.40.
At Monday's close the S&P 500 stood 1.8% below its all-time high, which was set early in June 2026.
The small-cap Russell 2000 rose 0.8% to 3,004.40, closing above the 3,000 level for the first time ever.

value.oil_driver: US-Iran negotiations on ending their war
value.reopen: first session after a three-day Juneteenth weekend

text: Stocks drifted through a mixed session as oil prices eased and falling Big Tech shares weighed on the market. Oil fell roughly 3% following negotiations between the United States and Iran on ending their war, while rising Treasury yields in the bond market also pressured equities. Trading resumed after a three-day weekend for the Juneteenth holiday.

value.alphabet_driver: concerns over AI-talent departures to competitors

text: Mega-cap technology shares led the Nasdaq lower. Per Yahoo Finance's session wrap, Alphabet fell about 5% amid concerns over AI-talent departures to competitors, and Amazon, Microsoft, and Meta Platforms also closed lower.

value.development: reported encouraging progress toward ending the US-Iran conflict
value.roadmap: committee reported to have agreed to a roadmap toward a final deal within 60 days

text: Investors weighed reports of encouraging progress in negotiations aimed at ending the US-Iran conflict, with a high-level committee reported to have agreed to a roadmap toward a final deal within 60 days after weekend talks in Switzerland. The diplomatic progress coincided with lower oil prices.

value.us_asymmetry: Europe traded a normal session on Friday June 19; Juneteenth closed only US venues, so the European daily move is measured against June 19 while the US June 22 move is measured against June 18

text: European equity markets traded a normal regular session on Monday, June 22, 2026, with live closing prints and full volume on the LSEG FTSE 100 daily table (about 1.06 billion shares) and dated June 22 closes across the Trading Economics Euro Area and Germany pages. Because European venues do not observe Juneteenth, they traded a full session on Friday, June 19, so the June 22 daily moves are measured against the June 19 close, one session more recent than the US benchmark, whose June 22 move is measured against June 18.

stoxx_europe_600.basis: dpa newswire +0.58%; Trading Economics dated wrap +0.7% to about 640; vendor delta disclosed

euro_stoxx_50.basis: Trading Economics dated wrap +0.4% to 6,320; CFD-settle math implies about 6,315; single-vendor, CFD-tracked

dax.basis: Trading Economics Germany June 22 news settle 25,140 / +154 / +0.62%

ftse_100.basis: Investors Chronicle/LSEG precise historical close; dpa +0.72%

market_session: European regular close on 2026-06-22; FTSE 100 at the 16:35 BST London close

calculation.method: FTSE 100: June 22 close 10,437.85 minus June 19 close 10,363.27 equals +74.58 points (+0.72%); DAX +154 points to 25,140 (+0.62%) vs June 19; CAC 40 -21.03 to 8,400.11 (-0.25%); continental percentages corroborated by dpa and Trading Economics

text: European equities closed mostly higher on Monday, June 22, 2026. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 ended around 640, up 0.58% on the dpa close wrap (Trading Economics put the same close near 640, +0.7%, and characterised it as a record high). The EURO STOXX 50 added about 0.4% to roughly 6,320 on the Trading Economics dated wrap (its CFD settle implies about 6,315). Germany's DAX rose 154 points (+0.62%) to 25,140, and the UK's FTSE 100 gained 74.58 points (+0.72%) to a precise 10,437.85 from Friday's 10,363.27. France's CAC 40 was the lone major decliner, off 21.03 points (-0.25%) to 8,400.11, weighed by luxury. Daily moves are measured against the June 19 close.

market_session: London regular session close, Monday 2026-06-22 (16:35 BST)

calculation.method: close 10,437.85 minus prior close 10,363.27 equals 74.58 points; 74.58 / 10,363.27 equals 0.72 percent

text: The FTSE 100 is the cycle's one precise official close: on the Investors Chronicle/LSEG historical daily table it opened 10,363.58, traded a 10,346.63 to 10,443.16 range, and closed 10,437.85 on volume of 1,061,872,669 shares, up 74.58 points (+0.72%) from Friday's 10,363.27. UK banks led the advance, with NatWest, Barclays and Lloyds each up about 4% per the dpa wrap.

subject.name: US-Iran peace progress and weak oil lift European equities

value.primary_support: positive progress in US-Iran peace negotiations and weak oil prices easing inflation concerns
value.iran_us: Iran said it made progress in the start of talks with the US over a permanent peace deal shortly after both parties signed a memorandum of understanding

text: European shares rose on Monday on positive progress in US-Iran peace negotiations and easing inflation concerns from weak oil prices, per the dpa close wrap. Trading Economics added that Iran reported progress in the start of talks with the US over a permanent peace deal shortly after both sides signed a memorandum of understanding, reinforcing the view that energy prices should stay lower and soften pro-inflationary risks. The de-escalation and lower-oil mix supported the broad indices, leaving France's luxury-heavy CAC 40 as the only major decliner.

value.rationale: eurozone sovereign yields pulled back, aiding the credit outlook

text: Financials and miners led the advance. The dpa wrap reported UK lenders NatWest, Barclays and Lloyds each up about 4%, while Trading Economics noted eurozone banks Santander, BBVA and Nordea rising around 2% as eurozone sovereign yields pulled back and improved the credit outlook; the Trading Economics Euro Stoxx Banks gauge closed up 1.00% for June 22. The single-stock percentages are approximate and carried at medium confidence.

gainers.Infineon_basis: dpa: about 5% on a humanoid-robot collaboration with VinRobotics; Trading Economics: +4.84% to 85.94 (Jun/22) and a separate news item +6.09%, framed as part of the AI-infrastructure rally

text: On single stocks, Infineon was the standout gainer, up roughly 5 to 6% - the dpa wrap tied the move to a humanoid-robot collaboration with VinRobotics, while Trading Economics logged Infineon +4.84% to 85.94 (and a DAX news item at +6.09%) as part of the AI-infrastructure rally; the differing rationales are disclosed. Other German gainers included Zalando (+4.12%), MTU Aero Engines (+1.65%) and Siemens (+1.64%). Luxury was the day's soft spot and the main drag on the CAC 40: Hermes fell about 6%, LVMH 3.59%, and EssilorLuxottica 2% to 4%, amid muted Moncler guidance and recent broker downgrades. SAP (-1.85%), Deutsche Telekom (-1.80%) and Scout24 (-1.86%) were among the DAX losers. Single-stock percentages are carried at medium confidence.

value.china_hong_kong_basis: Hong Kong and mainland China reopened after the Friday 2026-06-19 Tuen Ng/Dragon Boat market holiday; their daily moves compare with Thursday 2026-06-18
value.japan_korea_basis: Japan and South Korea used normal Monday local sessions, measured against Friday 2026-06-19 closes

text: Monday, June 22, 2026 was a regular trading day for the referenced Asia-Pacific cash-equity markets. Hong Kong and mainland China reopened after the Friday, June 19 Tuen Ng/Dragon Boat holiday, so Hong Kong and Shanghai daily moves are measured against June 18, while Japan and South Korea are measured against their normal June 19 closes.

nikkei_225.basis: cash close; AP rounds to +1.6%, MarketWatch reports +1.5%

kospi.basis: Yonhap close bulletin; AP rounds to +0.7%

shanghai_composite.basis: WSJ historical table; AP rounds to +1.8%

calculation.method: Nikkei: 72,353.96 minus 1,103.90 implies a prior close near 71,250.06 and +1.55%; KOSPI: 9,114.55 minus 62.13 equals 9,052.42 and +0.69%; Hang Seng: 23,768.52 minus 23,924.81 equals -156.29 and -0.65%; Shanghai: 4,163.10 minus 4,090.48 equals +72.62 and +1.78%

text: Asia-Pacific equity markets closed mixed on Monday, June 22, 2026. The Nikkei 225 rose 1,103.90 points (+1.55%) to 72,353.96, the KOSPI rose 62.13 points (+0.69%) to 9,114.55, and the Shanghai Composite rose 72.62 points (+1.78%) to 4,163.10. Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell 156.29 points (-0.65%) to 23,768.52. AP's broader regional wrap also put Australia's S&P/ASX 200 down 0.1% to 8,816.10, Taiwan's Taiex up 2.8%, and India's Sensex up 0.5%.

value.vendor_basis_note: Trading Economics CFD-tracked JP225 item says +1,128 points / +1.58% to 72,379, while AP/MarketWatch cash close is 72,353.96; report uses AP/MarketWatch for close precision and Trading Economics only as directional corroboration

calculation.method: MarketWatch/AP cash close and AP rounded mover percentages; Trading Economics JP225 source is CFD-derived and therefore not used for cash close precision

text: Japan's Nikkei 225 closed at a fresh all-time high of 72,353.96 on Monday, June 22, up 1,103.90 points or 1.55%. AP attributed the record close to technology stocks fueled by enthusiasm over the global AI boom, with SoftBank up 1.9% and Tokyo Electron up 3.2%. Trading Economics independently showed the same direction but on a CFD-tracked JP225 basis (+1.58% to 72,379), so the report uses AP/MarketWatch for the cash-index close.

calculation.method: Yonhap close bulletin gives KOSPI 9,114.55 up 62.13 points; 62.13 divided by implied previous close 9,052.42 equals 0.69%; AP corroborates rounded +0.7% and record-close framing

text: South Korea's KOSPI closed at a record 9,114.55 on Monday, June 22, up 62.13 points or 0.69%. AP corroborated the record-close framing and said the gain was helped by AI-related shares, with memory chip maker SK Hynix up 5.6%.

value.holiday_basis: Both markets reopened after the Friday 2026-06-19 Dragon Boat/Tuen Ng holiday, so the comparison close is 2026-06-18

market_session: Hong Kong and Shanghai regular cash-equity closes, Monday 2026-06-22

calculation.method: Shanghai Composite: 4,163.10 close minus 4,090.48 prior listed close equals +72.62 points (+1.78%); Hang Seng: 23,768.52 close minus 23,924.81 prior listed close equals -156.29 points (-0.65%). HKEX calendar confirms June 19 Tuen Ng/Dragon Boat closure basis.

text: Greater China markets diverged after reopening from the June 19 Tuen Ng/Dragon Boat holiday. The Shanghai Composite closed at 4,163.10, up 72.62 points or 1.78% from the prior listed June 18 close, while the Hang Seng closed at 23,768.52, down 156.29 points or 0.65% from its prior listed June 18 close.

subject.name: US-Iran progress lowers oil stress while AI hardware leads North Asia

value.macro_support: Oil prices eased as US-Iran talks progressed, improving risk appetite
value.breadth_constraint: Elevated US-rate expectations limited broader enthusiasm
value.sector_leadership: AI and semiconductor-linked stocks led Japan and South Korea; Hong Kong and Australia lagged

market_session: Asia-Pacific regular sessions and early US premarket context, Monday 2026-06-22
text: The Asia session's macro support came from easing oil stress as US-Iran talks showed progress: AP reported Brent down about 2% to $79.02 and US crude down 74 cents to $75.11 early Monday. LGT also said most Asian equity markets rose as easing concern over US-Iran tensions improved risk appetite, though elevated US-rate expectations limited breadth. The leadership was narrower than the headline suggests: AI and semiconductor-linked shares drove Japan and Korea records, while Hong Kong and Australia lagged.

value.us_reopen: first US fixed-income and equity session after the three-day Juneteenth weekend (June 19 holiday; June 20-21 weekend)
value.comparison_basis: US daily moves measured vs Thursday 2026-06-18
value.dominant_driver: conclusion of first-round US-Iran talks in Switzerland with a 60-day roadmap, easing the Middle East risk premium

text: June 22, 2026 was a normal Monday global macro session and the first US fixed-income and equity session after the three-day Juneteenth weekend (June 19 holiday, June 20-21 weekend), so US rate and equity moves are measured against Thursday, June 18; the official Treasury par-yield CSV read live on June 23 has its newest row dated 06/22/2026 with no 06/19-06/21 row in between. The day's macro tape was set by the conclusion of the first round of US-Iran talks in Switzerland with a 60-day roadmap toward a final deal, which eased the Middle East risk premium, layered on a still-hawkish Fed backdrop.

calculation.method: read official Treasury par-yield CSV live; 10Y minus 2Y; change vs the 06/18/2026 row (no 06/19-06/21 row exists)

text: US Treasury par yields backed up across the curve on June 22, closing at 4.24% (2Y), 4.25% (3Y), 4.29% (5Y), 4.39% (7Y), 4.51% (10Y), 4.97% (20Y) and 4.95% (30Y) - a roughly +5 to +6 bp parallel rise from the June 18 pre-holiday close (2Y 4.19%, 10Y 4.46%, 30Y 4.90%) with the 2s10s slope unchanged at +27 bp, a bear shift rather than a steepening or flattening; the long end kept its small kink with the 20Y (4.97%) sitting 2 bp above the 30Y (4.95%). The yield back-up was the day's rates headwind for equities, weighing on the rate-sensitive Nasdaq.

value.high_basis: near highest since May 2025; fresh ~13-month high on June 23
value.policy_context: markets positioned for further Fed tightening, not cuts; Deutsche Bank and BofA Global Research both forecast a September rate hike after last week's hawkish Fed

calculation.method: vendor DXY daily close (June 22) with the June 23 spot as the carry cross-check

text: The U.S. Dollar Index closed June 22 around 101.0 and pushed to roughly 101.17 on June 23, a fresh high and its strongest since May 2025; the move is policy-driven, with May CPI back at 4.2%, the fed funds rate at 3.75%, markets positioned for further Fed tightening rather than cuts, and both Deutsche Bank and BofA Global Research now carrying a September hike after last week's hawkish Fed - this week's PCE print is the next catalyst. A firm dollar alongside rising yields is the tightening-leaning half of the June 22 macro picture.

value.reversal_driver: Vance signaled 'great progress' in the Switzerland talks and the Strait of Hormuz kept flowing, reversing an early spike driven by Trump's strike threat and Iran's Hormuz-closure claim
value.supply_leg: US Treasury issued a 60-day license authorizing production, delivery and sale of Iranian oil, including US imports payable in dollars
value.wti_contract_note: expiring July WTI rolled to the August contract

calculation.method: June 22 futures settlement prices; intraday path from same-day reporting

text: Crude fell hard on June 22: Brent settled down 3.3% at $77.90 and WTI lost 2.3% to settle at $74.82, both near three-month lows. Prices first spiked to about $82.30 early Monday on Trump's threat to restart strikes and Tehran's renewed Strait of Hormuz closure claim, then reversed as Vance signaled 'great progress' in the Switzerland talks and the strait kept flowing; the US Treasury's 60-day license authorizing Iranian-oil production, delivery and sale - including US imports payable in dollars - added a supply-bearish leg, with Brent now down roughly 20% over the past month.

value.outcome: first round concluded June 22 with a roadmap aimed at a final deal within 60 days, building on the June 17 memorandum of understanding
value.governance: High Level Committee for political oversight, with working groups on nuclear, sanctions and dispute resolution
value.lebanon: US-Iran-Lebanon de-confliction cell to end hostilities in Lebanon
value.hormuz: MoU reopens the Strait of Hormuz toll-free for at least 60 days
value.iran_claims_araghchi: waivers for oil and petrochemical exports, lifting of the port blockade, release of some frozen assets, and a reconstruction and development plan
value.continuation: technical talks to continue through the week

text: Mediators Qatar and Pakistan said the US and Iran, meeting at the Burgenstock resort above Lake Lucerne, agreed on June 22 on a roadmap aimed at a final deal within 60 days, building on the June 17 MoU; the parties created a High Level Committee for political oversight with working groups on nuclear, sanctions and dispute resolution, plus a US-Iran-Lebanon de-confliction cell, and set technical talks to continue through the week. The MoU reopens the Strait of Hormuz toll-free for at least 60 days, and Iran's FM Abbas Araghchi said Tehran secured waivers for oil and petrochemical exports, a lifted port blockade, the release of some frozen assets and a reconstruction plan - the mechanism that converted a war-risk premium into a supply-normalization trade.

value.talks_tone: constructive but tense, ran into the early hours
value.trump_threat: publicly threatened to hit Iran very hard again even as Vance negotiated
value.hormuz_dispute: Iran's IRGC declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again Saturday; US military denied it (Iran does not control the Strait of Hormuz); Iran's Tasnim said the strait stays shut until a Lebanon ceasefire and oil-sanctions relief
value.lebanon: Israeli strikes reported to have killed at least 20 on Saturday (Lebanon's NNA); Araghchi called the Lebanon de-confliction mechanism the deal's first real test
value.analyst_caveats: ING flagged very real risks of a flare-up during the 60-day ceasefire; Quantum Strategy's David Roche warned the apparent oil abundance reflects inventory/tanker liquidation, not recovered production

text: The de-escalation is real but fragile, which is why the oil move was a partial repricing rather than a collapse: the talks were 'constructive but tense' and ran into the early hours, Trump publicly threatened to 'hit Iran very hard again' even as Vance negotiated, and Iran's IRGC had declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again Saturday - a claim the US military denied ('Iran does not control the Strait of Hormuz'), with Iran's Tasnim saying the strait stays shut until a Lebanon ceasefire and oil-sanctions relief. Lebanon stayed the live flashpoint, with Israeli strikes reported to have killed at least 20 on Saturday and Araghchi calling the Lebanon de-confliction mechanism the deal's 'first real test'; ING flagged 'very real risks' of a flare-up and Quantum Strategy's David Roche warned the apparent oil abundance reflects inventory and tanker liquidation rather than recovered production. These items are evolving and carried at medium confidence.

market_session: regular US cash-equity session after Juneteenth/weekend break
text: The June 22, 2026 report date was a regular Monday U.S. cash-equity session after the Juneteenth and weekend break, so sentiment values can be read against a live U.S. closing tape rather than a no-session carry.

value.context: weaker Big Tech and lower oil prices; Dow and small caps positive

text: AP reported a mixed June 22 U.S. close: the S&P 500 slipped 0.4% to 7,472.79 and the Nasdaq Composite dropped 1.3% to 26,166.60 as Big Tech weakened, while the Dow rose 0.3% to 51,712.71 and the Russell 2000 gained 0.8% to 3,004.40.

subject.name: CNN Fear & Greed Index
subject.source_basis: public CNN endpoint mirror corroborated by MacroMicro

market_session: regular US cash-equity session sentiment reading
text: The public CNN Fear & Greed mirror showed a June 22 score of 34.74, rated fear, up from 31.60 on June 19 but below 41.57 on June 15. MacroMicro corroborated the June 22 level at 34.74.

market_session: Cboe published volatility-index CSV rows for the regular US session

calculation.method: VIX one-day percent change = (17.28 - 16.78) / 16.78 * 100 = 2.98%; curve compares Cboe close fields for VIX9D, VIX, VIX3M, and VIX6M on 2026-06-22

text: Cboe's VIX CSV showed VIX closing at 17.28 on June 22, up 0.50 points or about 2.98% from June 19. The same-date curve was upward sloping, with VIX9D at 16.29, VIX at 17.28, VIX3M at 19.76, and VIX6M at 22.15, showing higher stress but not a front-end panic inversion.

market_session: Cboe daily market statistics page accessed after the June 22 session
text: Cboe's daily market-statistics page showed total put/call at 0.92, index put/call at 1.08, ETP put/call at 1.15, equity put/call at 0.60, VIX put/call at 0.23, and SPX + SPXW put/call at 1.22. The split points to index and ETP hedging alongside still-call-heavy equity options flow.

market_session: latest AAII weekly survey available for the June 22 report date

calculation.method: Bull-bear spread = bullish 36.6% minus bearish 39.4% = -2.8 percentage points

text: AAII's latest sentiment post showed bullish sentiment at 36.6%, neutral sentiment at 24.1%, and bearish sentiment at 39.4%, leaving a bull-bear spread of -2.8 percentage points. Retail sentiment was still net bearish, but pessimism decreased by 8.3 percentage points from the prior survey.

market_session: latest fund-flow releases available for the June 22 report date
text: Fund-flow data were constructive: ICI reported $31.52 billion of long-term fund and ETF inflows for the week ended June 10, including $48.33 billion of ETF net issuance, while ETF.com's June 22 weekly article reported $119.83 billion of total ETF inflows led by $83.62 billion into U.S. equity ETFs. Large redemptions from SPY and VOO were vehicle-level rotation rather than broad equity withdrawal because the U.S. equity asset-class total was strongly positive.

value.cash_equity_tape: Nasdaq weakness and S&P softness offset by Dow and Russell gains
value.options: index and ETP hedging with equity call appetite
value.flows: large U.S. equity and international equity ETF inflows

market_session: regular US cash-equity session synthesis from latest available sentiment indicators
text: The best synthesis for June 22 is 'fear headline, rotation bid underneath': Fear & Greed remained in fear, VIX and index hedging rose, and Big Tech weighed on the Nasdaq, but the Dow and Russell 2000 gained, AAII pessimism eased, and ETF flows showed heavy equity allocation. The setup was fragile but not capitulation.
